event: Neolithic farming communities of the Starčevo culture settle in the fertile river valleys of present-day Kosovo.
The Vinča culture flourishes, leaving notable sites such as Vlashnjë and producing among the earliest figurines and pottery in the region.
Illyrian tribes, particularly the Dardani, emerge as the dominant population across the territory.
The Dardanians establish the Kingdom of Dardania, a powerful Illyrian polity centered on the region.
Rome defeats Macedon and begins expanding its influence into the Dardanian lands.
Dardania is incorporated into the Roman province of Moesia following Roman conquest of the Balkan interior.
The Roman town of Ulpiana is founded near modern Pristina, becoming an important mining and administrative center.
Emperor Diocletian reorganizes the Balkans and the region falls within the renamed province of Dardania.
A devastating earthquake destroys Ulpiana, which Emperor Justinian I later rebuilds as Justiniana Secunda.
Slavic tribes migrate into and settle across the region as Byzantine control weakens.
The region comes under the control of the First Bulgarian Empire during its Balkan expansion.
Byzantine Emperor Basil II reconquers the area after defeating Bulgaria, restoring imperial rule.
Serbian Grand Prince Stefan Nemanja seizes the region, beginning lasting incorporation into the medieval Serbian state.
Saint Sava establishes the autocephalous Serbian Orthodox Church, with key religious institutions soon rooted in the region.
Stefan Dušan is crowned Emperor of the Serbs, and the Patriarchate is seated at Peć, making the area a Serbian political and spiritual heartland.
The Battle of Kosovo is fought on Kosovo Polje between Serbian-led forces and the Ottoman Empire, a pivotal and legendary clash.
The Ottomans complete their conquest of the region, organizing it into the Sanjak of Vučitrn under Ottoman administration.
The Ottomans restore the Serbian Patriarchate of Peć, strengthening Orthodox religious life under their rule.
After a failed anti-Ottoman uprising, the Great Migration of the Serbs sees tens of thousands flee northward, while Albanian settlement increases.
The Ottomans abolish the Serbian Patriarchate of Peć, placing the church under Greek Phanariot control.
The League of Prizren is founded to defend Albanian-inhabited lands and promote Albanian national autonomy within the Ottoman Empire.
During the First Balkan War, Serbian and Montenegrin forces capture the region from the Ottomans.
The Treaty of London confirms the partition of the region between Serbia and Montenegro, ending Ottoman rule.
The region becomes part of the newly formed Kingdom of Serbs, Croats and Slovenes, later Yugoslavia.
Axis powers occupy the region during World War II, with most of it annexed to Italian-controlled Greater Albania.
Partisan forces incorporate the region into Tito's socialist Yugoslavia as the Autonomous Region of Kosovo and Metohija within Serbia.
Albanian student demonstrations demand greater autonomy and university rights, signaling rising national assertiveness.
Yugoslavia's new constitution grants Kosovo extensive autonomy as a Socialist Autonomous Province with near-republic powers.
Mass Albanian protests demanding republic status are violently suppressed by Yugoslav authorities.
Serbian leader Slobodan Milošević strips Kosovo of its autonomy, igniting deep ethnic tensions.
Kosovo Albanians declare an unofficial Republic of Kosovo and build parallel institutions in response to Serbian repression.
The Kosovo Liberation Army emerges and begins armed attacks against Serbian forces.
Full-scale war erupts between the Kosovo Liberation Army and Yugoslav security forces, causing mass displacement of civilians.
NATO launches a 78-day bombing campaign against Yugoslavia, ending the war and placing Kosovo under UN administration via Resolution 1244.
Kosovo holds its first general elections under UN administration, with Ibrahim Rugova later elected president.
Widespread inter-ethnic riots erupt, leaving many dead and numerous Serbian Orthodox sites damaged.
Kosovo unilaterally declares independence from Serbia on 17 February, recognized by the United States and many Western states.
Kosovo adopts a new constitution, and the EU deploys the EULEX rule-of-law mission to support its institutions.
The International Court of Justice rules that Kosovo's declaration of independence did not violate international law.
Kosovo and Serbia sign the Brussels Agreement to normalize relations, brokered by the European Union.
Kosovo signs a Stabilisation and Association Agreement with the EU, its first contractual relationship with the bloc.
Kosovo establishes its own armed forces by transforming the Kosovo Security Force, drawing protest from Serbia.
Albin Kurti's Vetëvendosje movement wins a landslide election, and Vjosa Osmani is elected president.
Kosovo formally applies for membership in the European Union amid ongoing tensions in the Serb-majority north.
The EU-brokered Ohrid Agreement outlines steps toward normalizing Kosovo-Serbia relations, though full implementation stalls.
The euro becomes the sole permitted currency for cash transactions, sharpening disputes with the Serb-majority north as Kosovo deepens European ties.
